behaviors.description: The review analyzer processes only lines that changed in the pull request, not the full file contents
behaviors.justification: This is a parsing/filtering logic concern within a single component. The failure mode (including unchanged lines in analysis) is testable by exercising the diff parsing function with known inputs and verifying the output contains only changed lines. The failure boundary is the parsing algorithm itself, not a module interaction point.
behaviors.test_description: Create a test diff with mixed changed and unchanged lines, pass it to the diff parser, verify the parsed review input contains exactly the changed lines and excludes unchanged lines. Verify via line number comparison.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: A Unit-level test exercises the parsing logic directly. Integration would only be needed if the failure actually occurs at the module handoff (30% probability), but the core logic failure can be caught at Unit.

behaviors.description: Inline comments are posted to the exact line positions visible in GitHub's diff view on the pull request
behaviors.justification: GitHub's diff position numbering is complex (based on hunk structure, not absolute file positions) and deterministic but opaque. A mock GitHub API would accept any position you calculate—you'd never discover off-by-one errors or special cases. Only calling the real GitHub API and visually verifying (or querying the real API for the actual position) reveals whether your position calculation matches GitHub's interpretation.
behaviors.test_description: Post a comment to a real pull request in a real GitHub repository, then query the real GitHub API or view the PR in the web UI to verify the comment appears at the exact intended line in the diff view. Test with edge cases like comments at hunk boundaries.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: System level is required to validate against real GitHub's position numbering. Integration-level fixtures with reference diffs could catch some logic errors (35% probability), but only System catches real GitHub surprises.

behaviors.description: The agent identifies and reports at least one real defect present in a pull request with a known introduced bug
behaviors.justification: Bug detection depends on semantic code understanding. A mock LLM either always detects all bugs or never detects any—it cannot simulate real judgment variation. Real LLMs show non-deterministic behavior: running the same test 100 times may catch the bug 80 times and miss it 20 times. The failure mode is 'LLM reasoning was insufficient to understand this code defect', which is purely an LLM judgment failure. Unit/Integration tests with static code analysis or regex patterns miss the core issue.
behaviors.test_description: Create a real PR in a real GitHub repository containing a known bug (e.g., missing null check, off-by-one error, unhandled exception). Execute the agent using a real LLM API (e.g., Claude, GPT-4). Verify that the agent's findings list includes at least one comment/finding that correctly describes the introduced bug. Run multiple times if needed to account for LLM non-determinism.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Agentic level is necessary because correctness depends on the real LLM's ability to reason about the code. Lower levels cannot model the semantic understanding required.

behaviors.description: Every finding listed in the top-level PR summary comment has a matching inline comment posted at the relevant line
behaviors.justification: The failure is data inconsistency between two separately generated outputs: inline comments and the summary comment. Testing the inline comment generator alone (Unit) won't catch if the summary builder drops a finding. Testing the summary builder alone won't catch if inline comments failed to post. The failure appears at the module boundary—when data flows from comment generation through to summary aggregation. Integration testing both modules together with mocked GitHub API captures this data-flow failure.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the GitHub API for posting comments. Generate the full set of inline comments for a review, then generate the summary comment. Extract all findings from the summary comment text. Verify that each finding in the summary corresponds to exactly one inline comment. Check for both false positives (summary lists findings not commented) and false negatives (comments posted but not in summary).
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Integration testing of multiple modules wired together catches the boundary failure. A Unit test of either module alone would pass even if the other module fails to send data (Unit confidence 0.25 is secondary).

behaviors.description: The complete end-to-end sequence—reading a real PR diff, analyzing code with AI, posting inline comments, and creating a summary comment—completes without hanging, errors, or manual intervention
behaviors.justification: This tests orchestration of multiple steps in sequence: read PR from GitHub → analyze with LLM → post inline comments to GitHub → post summary. At least one step is Agentic (LLM analysis). The failure modes are deadlocks, race conditions, or state inconsistencies that only surface when all steps run together in order. Testing fewer steps (Unit, Integration, Agentic in isolation) won't catch orchestration-level failures like 'comments post before summary is ready' or 'LLM analysis times out blocking comment posting'.
behaviors.test_description: Create a real GitHub pull request with known code defects. Execute the full agent pipeline against the real PR, from reading the PR diff through posting the final summary comment. Verify: (1) no errors or hangs occur, (2) the process completes within a timeout, (3) all inline comments are posted, (4) the summary comment appears, (5) at least one defect is identified (per behavior 3), and (6) findings match comments (per behavior 4).
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Workflow level is needed because the failure is in orchestration across multiple steps, including an Agentic step. An Integration-level test with mocked GitHub and LLM might pass but fail on real systems (System concerns at 0.25). Workflow encompasses the orchestration.
